experiment: Measure score tie-margin between top-K and top-(K+1) at iter_0 untrained router

setup.input: 8192 tokens from train.bin[0:8192] (first sample of iter 1)
setup.router: first MoE layer (block 1), K=8 topk over 144 experts

interpretation: At iter_0 with randomly initialized router (init_std=0.006 in 144-expert space), the top-8 / top-9 score margin is below 1 bf16 ULP for 98.89% of tokens.
Any 1-ULP numerical difference in intermediate computation (F.linear cuBLAS tiling, sigmoid precision, score+bias ordering) can flip which expert ranks 8th vs 9th for nearly every token.
This explains WHY nano and ref diverge in routing from iter_1 even with bitwise-identical weights and inputs. But it does NOT establish the magnitude of the resulting loss-gap floor.
overclaim_correction_20260423: Earlier commits (745b173, 6bc101d, 50a3551) jumped from this mechanism to the conclusion that +0.005 nat is 'the bf16 physical floor' for nano-vs-ref alignment. That is not supported. This finding shows only that divergence is inevitable; the floor VALUE of the gap is not measured. Counter-evidence: v6-vs-v10 (both nano, different code, same seed) diff ≈ 0.0001, which is 50× smaller than the nano-vs-ref 0.005 gap. If bf16 topk-boundary noise were the whole story, two same-seed nano runs would also differ by ~0.005. The fact that they don't suggests the 0.005 gap contains a systematic component. Proper floor measurement requires nano-trained-with-seed-A vs nano-trained-with-seed-B (both different from ref's seed) — that experiment has not been run.
mitigation_options: (a) Train router in fp32 only (not bf16 autocast) — already done for scores

(b) Use fp32 residual stream → router input more precise. ABLATION RESULT (fp32_residual_ablation.json): no effect — the 98.9% bf16-ambiguity rate is driven by WEIGHT-init score clustering, not input precision.
(c) Bit-exact F.linear kernel matching Megatron's cuBLAS path — complex but possible
(d) Further investigation pending seed-swap floor measurement
